coredata.dc:title: Internal electric field promoted NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr Z-scheme heterojunction with rich oxygen vacancies for efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and reduction of Cr (VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Junfeng
Zhang, Ying
Wang, Yanan
Luo, Suyue
Ma, Chang
Zhang, Hao
Guo, Minghui
coredata.dc:description: It is important to prepare efficient photocatalysts for the degradation of antibiotics and heavy metals. In this study, NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts with excellent performance were constructed. NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr form a Z-type heterojunction system with pyrrole nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) as charge transfer channels. The internal electric field formed in NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr greatly facilitated electron transfer. The more easily broken Bi-O bonds were utilized in the synthesis to introduce rich oxygen vacancies (OVs) into NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr to promote molecular oxygen activation. Meanwhile, silver nanorods were reduced in the synthesis to enhance the photodegradation performance. The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) and reduction of Cr (VI) were enhanced by about 4 and 6 times. The factors affecting the removal effects of TC and Cr (VI) were analyzed. The catalysts were also tested to have good stability and potential for application in real wastewater. The reactive oxygen species (ROS), which play a dominant role in the photodegradation of TC and Cr (VI) by NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr, were tested, and a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed.

head.ce:title: Internal electric field promoted NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr Z-scheme heterojunction with rich oxygen vacancies for efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and reduction of Cr (VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin 150040, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is important to prepare efficient photocatalysts for the degradation of antibiotics and heavy metals. In this study, NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts with excellent performance were constructed. NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr form a Z-type heterojunction system with pyrrole nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) as charge transfer channels. The internal electric field formed in NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr greatly facilitated electron transfer. The more easily broken Bi-O bonds were utilized in the synthesis to introduce rich oxygen vacancies (OVs) into NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr to promote molecular oxygen activation. Meanwhile, silver nanorods were reduced in the synthesis to enhance the photodegradation performance. The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) and reduction of Cr (VI) were enhanced by about 4 and 6 times. The factors affecting the removal effects of TC and Cr (VI) were analyzed. The catalysts were also tested to have good stability and potential for application in real wastewater. The reactive oxygen species (ROS), which play a dominant role in the photodegradation of TC and Cr (VI) by NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr, were tested, and a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution is a growing and urgent problem. In particular, water contamination from antibiotics and heavy metals is becoming more and more serious [1]. TC as a common antibiotic widely used in various industries, is often discharged into water and is difficult to be degraded [2]. The highly toxic chromium (VI) is also widely present in the discharged industrial wastewater [3]. Therefore, an environmentally friendly and high-efficiency technology to purify the water environment needs to be developed. Among the various methods, photocatalysis is a highly efficient, sustainable and non-polluting technology. Semiconductor photocatalysis is one of the most promising technologies that utilise green and sustainable solar energy to reduce pollutants by oxidation. Photocatalysis can degrade TC as well as reduce Cr (VI) to low toxicity Cr (III) [4].

BiOBr is a bismuth-based semiconductor material with a unique layered structure. The unique layered structure of BiOBr, in which the [Bi2O2] slab is interspersed with two bromine atoms slab, facilitates the formation of an electrostatic field and thus promotes carrier separation [5]. Therefore, BiOBr is commonly used as a photocatalyst. However, BiOBr has a limited light response range and low photogenerated charge migration efficiency, resulting in unsatisfactory photodegradation [6]. Oxygen vacancies (OVs) enhance surface oxygen adsorption, which effectively improves degradation. At the same time, OVs can reduce the semiconductor band gap and enhance the catalyst's ability to capture visible light [7]. OVs in semiconductors can inhibit electron-hole pair complexation by preferentially trapping separated electrons. Therefore, the synthesis of BiOBr containing oxygen vacancies (OVs) can improve the absorption of visible light and promote exciton dissociation [8]. However, the photocatalytic effect of single component photocatalysts is not good enough, and the electron-hole separation efficiency is still low. Therefore, selecting suitable semiconductor materials to construct Z-scheme heterojunctions can overcome these drawbacks and provide the catalysts with good redox capabilities.

AgBr is a photocatalyst with strong visible light response. It has good photocatalytic activity due to d10 filled configuration of its Ag+ ion and suitable band gap [9]. Single AgBr is very unstable and decomposes easily in the presence of light [10]. However, loading it into other materials to form heterojunctions can prevent its photolysis and thus improve the stability [11,12]. Therefore, the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction using AgBr and BiOBr can promote charge transfer and improve the photocatalytic ability as well as photocatalytic stability. However, it remains a challenge to make a tight contact interface among BiOBr and AgBr to promote charge transfer. Many heterostructures have weak interfacial contacts unfavorable for charge transfer due to lattice loss or defects, etc [13]. Therefore, if a material can be found as an electron mediator and linked with BiOBr and AgBr to strengthen the interfacial contact, the photocatalytic activity and photogenerated carrier migration efficiency will be greatly enhanced [13,14].

Carbon dots (CDs) are new nanomaterials that have been extensively studied recently. CDs have the ability to enhance light absorption, store and transport electrical charges [15]. Due to their structural properties and abundant surface functional groups, they can be easily assembled with other materials [16]. CDs also have the ability to induce oxygen vacancies (OVs) and modulate the morphology of the material [6,17–19]. In addition, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) have more functional groups than CDs, which can enhance the catalytic ability when combined with semiconductors, and NCDs have better optical properties, which can further enhance the absorption ability of semiconductor photocatalysts in the full-spectrum band [19]. Therefore, the use of NCDs as electronic mediators for BiOBr and AgBr to construct Z-scheme heterojunctions that are more favorable for charge transfer is worth investigating in depth. Meanwhile, this Z-scheme interface charge transport channel has an internal electric field (IEF) [20]. This can modulate the photogenerated carriers at the heterojunction and improve the charge migration efficiency [21].

Based on the above analysis, the NCDs mediated BiOBr/AgBr Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts were synthesised in one step by a hydrothermal method in this study. During the synthesis process, OVs were introduced into BiOBr due to the action of ethylene glycol and NCDs. Surprisingly, silver nanorods were reduced, which drastically enhanced the photocatalytic activity. The electron transfer was also promoted due to the presence of IEF. The ability of the catalysts to remove TC, Cr (VI), and mixed TC-Cr (VI) solutions was researched. The factors affecting adsorption and photoreduction of Cr (VI) and the catalyst's ability to mineralize TC were explored. The practical application potential of the samples in various water bodies and the stabilisation of the photocatalysts were analyzed. Reactive oxygen species (ROS), which play a dominant role in the photocatalytic process, were identified. Scheme 1
The information of the experimental section is in the supporting information of Text S1-S7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of microstructure and chemical composition
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr, pure BiOBr, and pure AgBr was observed by SEM. As can be observed from Fig. 1b, pure BiOBr is a flower-like microsphere composed of nanosheets closely crossed. And the stacked irregular lumps in Fig. 1c are pure AgBr. In the SEM image (Fig. 1a) of NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr (0.4 ABC), it can be seen that the structure of the flower-like BiOBr composed of nanosheets becomes loose and there are lumps of AgBr attached to it. This may be due to the fact that AgNO3 added before heating reacts preferentially with KBr to form AgBr [10], which affects the generation of BiOBr later. This is also the reason why the solution turns yellowish green after adding AgNO3 in the synthesis experiment. Combined with the XRD findings, there are also fine silver nanorods interspersed in the gap between BiOBr and AgBr. This is because AgNO3 is reduced to Ag by the high temperature solvent heat of ethylene glycol [22]. The formation of silver nanorods is ascribed to the combined action of ethylene glycol and NCDs. The photocatalytic performance of 0.4 ABC is enhanced due to the surface plasmon resonance effect (SPR) of silver [23]. The TEM image of the NCDs in Fig. 1d shows circular dark dots. The morphology of 0.4ABC was further confirmed by TEM. Nanosheet stacked BiOBr and bulk AgBr can be seen in Fig. 1e, and tiny silver nanorods can be seen in the middle of them. Fig. 1f shows the silver nanorods observed after further magnification. The microstructure of 0.4 ABC was studied using HRTEM. The connected BiOBr and AgBr can be seen in Fig. 1g, and the measured lattice spacing of 0.32 nm corresponds to the (002) crystal face of the black dark dotted NCDs [24]. It indicates that the NCDs were successfully loaded. The two semiconductors formed a tightly connected heterojunction interface [25]. The lattice spacings of 0.277 nm and 0.289 nm in Fig. 1h belong to the (100) and (200) crystal planes of BiOBr and AgBr, respectively. The tight binding of BiOBr and AgBr is further demonstrated, which facilitates the fast migration of photoelectrons [26]. In addition, the EDS patterns (Fig. 1i) of NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr proved the presence for Bi, Br, O, and Ag elements, demonstrating successful synthesis of heterojunction composites.

The XRD plot in Fig. 2a shows the crystal structures of 0.4ABC, AB, BiOBr and AgBr. It can be seen that the derivative peaks at 10.9, 21.9, 25.2, 31.7, 32.2, 40.5, 46.2, and 57.2° correspond to the (001), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), and (212) of BiOBr (JCPDS No.85–0862) Crystallization. The diffraction peaks of the prepared pure AgBr at 26.7, 30.9, 44.3 and 55.0° correspond to the (111), (200), (220) and (222) crystal planes of the AgBr (JCPDS No. 79–0149). The crystalline phases of BiOBr and AgBr can clearly observed in the XRD plots of the nanocomposites AB and 0.4 ABC. A peak belonging to the Ag (111) crystal plane was also detected at 38.1° (JCPDS No. 87–0717). The combination of SEM and TEM results further confirmed the generation of Ag nanorods in the catalyst. Previous studies in which only Ag nanoparticles were synthesized were easily affected by other substances leading to difficulty in detecting the characteristic peaks of Ag [27–29]. However, the Ag nanorods synthesized in this experiment can be clearly detected from XRD. The XRD patterns of NCDs and catalysts containing different mass percentages of AgBr are shown in Fig. 2b. There is a broad peak at 20–30° in the XRD pattern of NCDs attributed to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon [30]. 0.2ABC, 0.4ABC, 0.6ABC, 0.8ABC all match well with the crystalline phases of BiOBr, AgBr, and all of them can be detected as Ag diffraction peaks. As the percentage of AgBr increases, a significant increase in the characteristic AgBr peak located at 30.9° can be seen.

The OVs in BiOBr, AB, 0.4ABC were analyzed using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) at room temperature. In Fig. 2c, a strong signal of OVs can be detected in AB and BiOBr at the position g=2.003. It indicates that the binding with AgBr promotes the production of OVs, which can promote exciton dissociation [8]. Furthermore, the strongest EPR peak was observed for the OVs of 0.4 ABC, indicating that the addition of NCDs in the synthesis facilitates the generation of OVs. This may be due to the solvent heat of ethylene glycol enhancing the action of NCDs to break the Bi-O bond [6]. Therefore, the NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr synthesized by one-step solvothermal synthesis had rich oxygen vacancies, enhancing the adsorption and transformation of oxygen species and promoting the generation of ROS [31].

The surface areas of 0.4ABC, AB and BiOBr and the corresponding pore size profiles were analyzed by BET tests. The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isothermal curves and the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model for 0.4ABC, AB and BiOBr are shown in Fig. 2d. All the samples reveal type IV isotherms and h3 hysteresis loops, which indicates the presence of mesoporous structure in the samples [32]. The presence of mesoporous structures can be seen in the pore size distribution graph of the samples. The specific surface areas of 0.4ABC, AB and BiOBr were obtained to be 20.1, 15.0 and 10.2 m2/g. So the addition of AgBr increases the surface area of the catalyst. The surface area of 0.4 ABC is almost two times that of BiOBr. This is due to the fact that AgBr and NCDs addition leads to the loosening of the flower-like structure of BiOBr and the contribution of the reduced silver nanorods [33], which corresponds to the results of electron microscopy. As a result, 0.4ABC has more active sites, which contributes to the enhancement of adsorption and degradation of pollutants as well as ROS production [34], which corresponds to the results of the photocatalytic activity experiments.

Figure S1 shows the XPS profile of the NCDs and it can be seen that the prepared carbon dots consisted of C, O, and N, indicating that the nitrogen-doped carbon dots were successfully prepared. In Fig. S1a, it can be seen that the C1s profile of NCDs consists of three peaks centered on C-C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (285.9), and CO (287.6 eV). The C-N bond originates from the amino group on the surface of the NCDs [35]. The O 1 s profile of the NCDs (Fig. S1b) is shown in Figs. ∼ 531.0 and 532.6 eV centered on two peaks from CO and C-O, respectively [36]. This can prove that NCDs have carboxyl groups on the surface, and NCDs with carboxyl groups have higher photocatalytic activity [37]. The pyrrole nitrogen located at 400.1 eV can be detected in the N 1 s pattern of NCDs (Fig. S1 c). The formation of pyrrole nitrogen is due to the addition of ethylenediamine during the synthesis of NCDs. Doping the carbon dots with this chemical form of pyrrole nitrogen is more helpful to promote the photocatalytic reaction [38].

The full-scan XPS spectra of BiOBr, AB, and 0.4ABC all show peaks of elements Bi, Br, and O. AB and 0.4ABC clearly have more peaks of Ag than BiOBr. The C 1 s spectra of BiOBr and AB show peaks with binding energies of 284.8, 286.2, and 288.8 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-O, CO. In Fig. 3a, pure BiOBr shows two peaks centered at 160.0 and 165.3 eV, belonging to Bi 4 f 7/2 and Bi 4 f 5/2 of Bi 3+ [39]. When BiOBr is compounded with AgBr to form a heterojunction, it can be seen that the two peaks of Bi 4 f in AB are shifted to 159.5 and 164.8 eV, respectively. Compared to BiOBr, Bi 4 f in AB is reduced by 0.5 eV. This suggests that upon formation of the heterojunction, the electron density in BiOBr is increased and the electrons migrate from AgBr into BiOBr [40,41]. However, the two peak binding energies of Bi 4 f in 0.4ABC with added carbon dots were 159.3 and 164.6 eV, which decreased by 0.2 eV compared to AB. this suggests that the electron density in BiOBr becomes higher due to the presence of NCDs, and electrons migrate from AgBr to BiOBr via NCDs. Fig. 3b shows that the two peaks of Br 3d of BiOBr are located at 68.9 and 70.0 eV. Br 3d in AB is negatively shifted by 0.4 eV compared to BiOBr. The two peaks of Br 3d in 0.4ABC are located at the same position as that of AB and are located at 68.5 and 69.5 eV. The O 1 s of BiOBr (Fig. 3c) could be fitted as the two peaks at 530.6 and 532.4 eV correspond to lattice oxygen and chemisorbed oxygen in BiOBr, respectively [6]. And the extra peaks of 531.9 and 531.7 eV in AB and 0.4ABC, respectively, are due to the oxygen vacancies generated in AB and 0.4ABC [42]. This corresponds to the EPR test results. The O 1 s peaks in AB and 0.4ABC are negatively shifted by 0.3 and 0.4 eV compared to BiOBr, respectively. This is the same change as in Bi 4 f. The peaks centered at 368.0 and 374.0 eV shows in the Ag 3d pattern of AB in Fig. 3d. And the two peaks of Ag 3d in 0.4ABC produce positive shifts to 368.1 and 374.1 eV, respectively, which are elevated by 0.1 eV. This further indicates that there is a transfer of electrons from AgBr to NCDs [43]. Combined with the above changes, it can be inferred that electrons are transferred from AgBr to BiOBr via NCDs in the 0.4ABC heterojunction. This proves that there may be a built-in electric field between BiOBr and AgBr and provides a basis for the Z-type heterojunction mechanism [40].

Fig. 4a shows the different catalysts' UV-DRS spectra, pure BiOBr has the weakest absorption of light showing an absorption edge of 460 nm. AgBr has excellent visible absorption in the full spectral range with an absorption edge at 500 nm. However, the photostability of pure AgBr is poor, resulting in a weak photocatalytic ability [44]. When BiOBr is combined with AgBr, it not only enhances the stability of AgBr, but also improves the photoabsorption ability of BiOBr. Therefore, the light absorption of AB was improved over BiOBr in the full wavelength range. However, the samples with NCDs added had the strongest light absorption, such as the absorption edge of 0.4ABC was extended to 545 nm, which had the best photoresponsive ability. This may be a result of the combined influence of SPR effect of Ag nanorods and NCDs as photosensitisers [45]. The Eg of the samples in Fig. 4b were all calculated using Kubelka-Munk theory:

Since both BiOBr and AgBr are indirect semiconductors, the value of n is 2 [24]. Consequently, the BiOBr and AgBr have Eg of 2.20 eV and 2.37 eV.

Using Mott-Schottky (M-S) tested the semiconductor type and flat band potential (Efb) of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 4c, it can be obtained that BiOBr is an n-type semiconductor because its M-S curve has a positive slope. While AgBr in Fig. 4d shows a negative slope proving that it is a p-type semiconductor. The M-S curves of BiOBr and AgBr at different frequencies were tested, and the straight-line portions of the M-S plots for three different frequencies intersected at one point, resulting in a more accurate flat band potential (Efb) position [46]. The Efb of BiOBr and AgBr were measured to −0.88 and 2.19 V, respectively, using an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. According to Nernst formula:
(2)

the Efb of BiOBr and AgBr were calculated as −0.69 and 2.38 V (vs. NHE). Since the Efb of n-type and p-type semiconductors varies approximately between below 0.1–0.3 V (CBM) or above 0.1–0.3 V (VBM). So according to formula:

the ECB of BiOBr was adjusted to −0.59 V, while the EVB of AgBr was 2.48 V. Combining the Eg calculations of BiOBr and AgBr, their energy band structures were obtained (Fig. 4f). In addition, an inverted "V" shape curve with both positive and negative slopes can be seen in the M-S diagram of the composite photocatalyst 0.4ABC. This indicates that a p-n junction is formed between them [47]. For p-type semiconductors, the Fermi energy levels will be located near the valence band, while for n-type semiconductors, the Fermi energy levels will be located near the conduction band [48]. The ECB of BiOBr is calculated to be −0.59 V, while the EVB of AgBr is 2.48 V. In order to reach thermodynamic equilibrium when the two come into contact, electrons are transferred from AgBr to BiOBr. This eventually leads to a downward bending of the BiOBr energy band and an upward bending of the AgBr energy band, creating a new common Fermi energy level [49]. This band bending generates an IEF from BiOBr to AgBr, which greatly improves the photocatalytic activity [48].

EIS was used to test the interfacial charge transfer efficiency of the samples. In the Nyquist plot, if the interfacial charge transfer resistance is large, then the arc radius is larger [15]. Fig. 5a shows that pure BiOBr and AgBr have the largest radii, indicating that they have high interfacial charge transfer resistance. On the other hand, the resistance of AB was significantly reduced, which was attributed to the heterojunction and silver nanorods working together to promote the conductivity of the material [50]. In comparison, 0.4ABC has the smallest arc radius i.e. the lowest resistance, which suggests that NCDs further improve the interfacial charge transfer rate. As deduced from Fig. 5b, the interfacial resistance of the catalyst decreases with increasing AgBr to BiOBr mass ratio. Until the mass ratio reaches 0.4, the interfacial resistance of the catalyst reaches the lowest. However, too much AgBr leads to a higher resistance value, detrimental to charge transfer.

The transient photocurrent response curves of the catalysts at every 20 s on/off cycle were shown on Figs. 5c and 5d. Photocurrent response intensity of AB was higher compared with BiOBr. However, the transient photocurrent density of the catalyst with added NCDs was further improved with 0.4 ABC having the maximum photocurrent response intensity. This indicates that the sample with added NCDs has the highest electron-hole separation efficiency [51]. The photocurrent of 0.4 ABC is stronger than other catalysts with other mass ratios, which corresponds to the results of the EIS test as well as the results of the photocatalytic experiments.

The electron-hole complexation of the catalysts and the fluorescence lifetime of photocarrier were tested via photoluminescence spectroscopy and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. BiOBr has the strongest PL intensity in Fig. 5e, which indicates the high electron-hole complexation efficiency and low charge mobility of BiOBr [50,52]. The PL intensity of the sample after compounding AgBr was significantly reduced, which indicates that the heterojunction formed effectively inhibited the electron and hole complexation. While 0.4ABC has the lowest PL intensity and the most efficient electron migration efficiency. This indicates that the addition of NCDs formed a new electron transfer channel, which effectively improved the electron-hole complexation [53]. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of 0.4ABC, AB and BiOBr were fitted with a double exponential decay function (Fig. 5f). By the formula:

The average carrier lifetime (τα) of the catalysts was calculated, where A1 and A2 are the amplitudes, and τ1 and τ2 are the PL emission lifetimes. The average carrier lifetimes of 0.4ABC, AB and BiOBr were calculated to be 4.53 ns, 4.08 ns and 3.64 ns, respectively. The average carrier lifetime of AB is slightly longer than that of BiOBr, which is attributed to the formation of heterostructure [54]. And 0.4ABC has the longest average carrier lifetime, which suggests that the NCDs become electronic mediators to form new charge transfer channels and effectively prolong the carrier lifetime [55]. Combined with the results of EIS, i-t test, it is obtained that the Z-scheme heterojunction may be formed with NCDs as electron mediators in 0.4ABC [53,56,57], which effectively improves the charge transfer efficiency.

The catalysts were tested for photocatalytic degradation of TC under visible light. From the dark reaction stage, it is evident that the adsorption capacity of 0.4ABC on TC is the largest, which is due to the larger specific surface area of 0.4ABC, which can provide more active sites [10]. Blank experiments showed that the autophotolysis of TC could be negligible. As shown in Fig. 6a, 0.4ABC had the highest photocatalytic activity and was able to degrade 94% of TC in 60 min, which was considerably more efficient than the catalytic efficiency of BiOBr of 59%. The removal of TC by AB and AgBr was 83% and 77%, respectively. Although the degradation efficiency of AgBr is not low, it is poorly stabilized and will decompose automatically with long light exposure. In Fig. 6c, the removal of TC by 0.4 ABC is significantly larger than that by 0.2 ABC, 0.6 ABC and 0.8 ABC. This indicates that the catalyst is most effective at a mass ratio of AgBr to BiOBr is 0.4. As shown in Figs. 6b and 6d, the photocatalytic degradation kinetics conformed to the quasi-primary kinetics of:
(5)

C

= kt
where C0 is the initial concentration, Ct is the concentration at reaction time t, and k is the apparent rate constant. 0.4ABC has the highest apparent rate constant (k=0.0456 min−1), which is about 3.46 times higher than that of BiOBr (k=0.0132 min−1). The k-values for AB, AgBr, 0.2ABC, 0.6ABC, and 0.8ABC were respectively were 0.0273, 0.0234, 0.0255, 0.0350 and 0.0301 min−1, the magnitudes of which correspond to the photocatalytic degradation curves.

The TC degradation ability of 0.4ABC at different initial TC concentrations was explored. When the concentration of TC was changed from 20 mg/L to 50 mg/L, the degradation efficiency of the catalyst decreased by about 36%. On the one hand, it was because the higher concentration of TC caused visible light to be difficult to reach the catalyst surface, thereby decreasing the absorption of photons [58]. On the other hand, the higher concentration for TC and intermediates generated during the degradation process were adsorbed on the material surface, occupying part of the active sites [59].

There are many inorganic salts in practical applications that can have an effect on photocatalytic degradation [60], so the change of TC photodegradation efficiency in the presence of different ions 0.4ABC was explored. In Fig. 6f, the catalyst can still remove 90% of TC when

ions on the photodegradation efficiency of TC is negligible. However, the presence of Cl− decreases the TC removal rate to 82%, which is because Cl− competes with the catalyst for the active substance h+ thus reducing the oxidizing ability [7,28]. When
SO
4
2 −
ions were introduced into the solution, the removal of TC by the catalyst decreased to 80%. This may be due to the competition of
SO
4
2 −
ions with TC for the reaction sites of the catalyst, leading to a decrease in degradation efficiency [61]. The degradation efficiency on TC was significantly reduced when
HCO
3
−
ions were in solution. The degradation was slow after 30 min of light exposure, and only 73% of the TC could be degraded in the end. This is because
HCO
3
−
depletes reactive species such as hydroxyl radicals [4,62,63], resulting in the inhibition of the photocatalytic activity of 0.4ABC.

The degradation of TC was studied at different pH values. In Fig. 6g, the best removal of TC was achieved by 0.4 ABC when the solution was neutral. The removal rate was maintained above 80% at all pH values above 5, which proved that the catalyst had a high efficiency in the degradation of TC at most pH values. In addition, the removal rates were generally higher in alkaline environments than in acidic environments. This is due to the fact that high pH values favour the production of hydroxyl radicals [17,64].

Degradation of TC in deionised water, tap water and river water was used to examine the catalytic effect of the materials in real wastewater. As shown in Fig. 6h, 0.4ABC was still able to remove 82% of TC in tap water, but the removal rate decreased to 73% in river water. This is due to the high content of organic matter in the river water, which competes with the active sites of the catalyst, and some inorganic salt ions that inhibit the production of active substances [10,65]. However, the catalyst was still able to degrade more than 70% of TC, indicating that the catalyst has potential for practical application.

The degree of TC mineralisation is an important indicator of complete degradation. Fig. 6i shows the total organic carbon content at different degradation times. The TOC concentration of TC degraded by 0.4ABC decreased sharply before 80 min, and slowly decreased to 58% after 80 min. 0.4ABC mineralised TC continuously, which will eventually be converted into CO2,
NO
3
−
and
NH
4
+
[66,67].

Reduction experiments for Cr (VI) were performed at pH = 5 unless otherwise stated. In Fig. 7a, the Cr (VI) solution concentration without catalyst hardly changed under light. The dark reaction stage showed the best adsorption of Cr (VI) by 0.4 ABC, which facilitated the photocatalysis reaction. After 60 min of light exposure, the rate of reduction of Cr (VI) was 62%, 72%, 89% and 100% for AgBr, BiOBr, AB and 0.4ABC, respectively. It is noteworthy that 0.4ABC reduced Cr (VI) almost completely within 40 min. Fig. 7c presents the Cr (VI) reduction curves for catalysts with different AgBr mass percentages, and it is observed that 0.4ABC has the best reduction effect. The k values of reduced Cr (VI) for all samples are shown in Figs. 7b and 7d. The apparent rate of 0.4 ABC (0.0819 min−1) was 4.71 and 6.30 time more than pure BiOBr (k=0.0174) and AgBr (0.0130), respectively. Fig. 7e shows the UV-Vis absorption curves of Cr (VI) solutions reduced by 0.4ABC at different times. The insets therein are photographs of Cr (VI) solutions after color development reaction at different times. It can be seen that the purple-red color of the solution almost completely disappeared as the light exposure time grows. The 545 nm characteristic peak could not be detected in the solution after 40 min of illumination, which indicates that Cr (VI) has been completely removed.

In Fig. 7f, the fastest removal of Cr (VI) was observed at an initial concentration of 20 mg/l. The fastest reduction of 0.4ABC was observed at an initial concentration of 20 mg/L of Cr (VI). The 0.4 ABC removed 96% of the Cr (VI) in 60 min when the concentration was increased to 30 mg/L. However, the reduction of the catalyst was significantly much lower at Cr (VI) solution concentrations of 40 and 50 mg/L. This may be due to the limited active sites of the catalyst, and the excessive Cr (VI) reduced the adsorption and catalytic performance of the material when competing for the active sites [68,69].

Since pH is an important influencing factor for Cr (VI) removal, the reduction effect of 0.4ABC on Cr (VI) at different pH was explored. In Fig. 7 g, when the pH was 3 and 5, 0.4ABC could remove Cr (VI) completely within 30 min and 40 min. Whereas, when the pH was increased to 7, 9 and 11, the catalyst could only degrade 91%, 77% and 41% of Cr (VI). This indicates that the acidic condition favors the removal of Cr (VI). The adsorption of Cr (VI) by the catalyst was significantly higher at pH 3 and 5 than at pH 7, 9 and 11, as seen in the dark phase. In order to reveal this change, the zeta potential of 0.4 ABC at different pH was tested [70]. In Fig. S2, the surface of 0.4ABC was electroneutral at pH 3 and 5, which was able to adsorb Cr (VI) oxyanion efficiently. zeta potential of 0.4ABC was 0 at pH 5.3, and the surface was electroneutral. At pH 7, 9 and 11, the surface of the sample was electronegative, which was detrimental to the attraction of Cr (VI) oxyanions due to electrostatic repulsion [71]. Hexavalent chromium exists mainly as Cr2O7 2- in acidic solutions and as CrO4 2- in neutral and alkaline solutions [72].

From the equation of Cr (VI) reduction to Cr (III), the Cr (OH)3 generated under alkaline conditions would be deposited on the catalyst surface, which is not favorable for further photocatalytic reaction [73,74]. Meanwhile, the standard reduction potential of Cr2O7 2-/Cr3+ (+1.36 eV vs. NHE) was much higher than that of CrO4 2-/Cr(OH)3 (-0.13 eV vs. NHE) [75]. Therefore, the reduction of Cr2O7 2- to Cr3+ is more thermodynamically feasible, so low pH is more favorable for Cr (VI) reduction.

In Fig. S3, the addition of
NO
3
−
, Cl− and
SO
4
2 −
had no effect in the reduction of Cr (VI). However, the reduction of Cr (VI) was reduced to 92% with the addition of
HCO
3
−
. Fig. 7h demonstrates the effect of 0.4ABC on the reduction of Cr (VI) in different water bodies. In deionised and tap water, 0.4 ABC after light exposure almost completely removed Cr (VI) from the water. In contrast, only 82% of Cr (VI) could be reduced in river water. This may be due to the presence of many pollutants and different ions competing for the reaction sites and active species in the actual river water, as well as the obstruction of visible light from reaching the catalyst surface [65,76]. These factors together lead to a decrease in the photoreduction of Cr (VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic removal of TC-Cr (VI) mixed solution
ce:section.ce:para: The effectiveness of the catalyst in removing mixed pollutants was explored. The pH of the mixture was 5 since TC would not degrade in strong acids and alkalis according to the experiment. In Fig. 7i, the autophotolysis of TC and Cr (VI) in the mixed solution is negligible, and neither of them undergoes redox reactions [68]. TC degradation in the mixed solution was 91%, compared to 86% for the single TC solution. The Cr (VI) was completely removed from the mixed solution in 30 minutes of light exposure, quicker than the 40 minutes for the single solution. the Cr (VI) reduction efficiency in the mixed solution was significantly accelerated. The faster photodegradation rate of 0.4ABC in the TC/Cr (VI) system may be attributed to the fact that the consumption of holes and electrons by TC and Cr (VI) indirectly facilitates the charge separation [77,78].

To examine the stabilisation of the photocatalysts, 0.4 ABC cycles were used to degrade TC and reduce Cr (VI). As shown in Fig. 8a, five cycling experiments were performed. In each cycle, dark adsorption was performed for 30 minutes and light reaction for 60 minutes. The removal of TC and Cr (VI) decreased in each cycle, but even after 5 cycles 0.4ABC was still able to degrade 79% of TC and reduce 89% for Cr (VI). The slight decrease in adsorption and catalytic activity could be attributed to the incomplete removal of TC intermediates adhering to the catalyst surface and reduced Cr (III) [71]. The XRD pattern of the catalyst after purging of TC and Cr (VI) can be seen in Fig. 8b as before unused. SEM images of the 0.4ABC photocatalyst after repeated use tests were tested in the support information in Figure S4. It can be seen that the 0.4ABC did not undergo morphological changes even after repeated use. The flower-like BiOBr, blocky AgBr, and Ag nanorods can be clearly seen in the figure. These results indicate that 0.4ABC has excellent photocatalytic stability and recyclability.

The XPS spectra of 0.4ABC after performing the cycling experiments were examined. The Bi 4 f, Br 3d, C 1 s, Ag 3d and O 1 s peaks were visible from the full XPS spectrum of 0.4ABC (Fig. 8c). This indicates that there is no change in the chemical composition of the catalyst. However, peaks of Cr 2p were additionally detected on the catalyst surface. The two peaks centred at 577.1 eV and 586.1 eV in the fine spectrum of Cr 2p in Fig. 8d represent the Cr 2p3/2 and Cr 2p1/2 peaks for Cr (III), respectively. This demonstrates that Cr (VI) on the surface of 0.4ABC was completely reduced to Cr (III) [71,79].

The major free radicals in the photodegradation of 0.4ABC were explored by reactive species capture experiments. Isopropanol (IPA), L(+)-histidine (L-his), silver nitrate (AgNO3), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were used as the free radicals for hydroxyl radicals (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), electrons (e−), holes (h+) and superoxide radicals (·
O
2
−
) scavengers [80]. As shown in Fig. 9a, the order of contributing roles of free radicals in TC degradation was 1O2 > ·
O
2
−
> h+ > e− > ·OH compared to the reaction system without scavengers. The role of active substances in the reduction of Cr (VI) was: e− > 1O2 > ·
O
2
−
> ·OH > h+. It could be inferred that e− plays a dominant role in the reduction of Cr (VI). It is worth noting that there are two pathways for the generation of 1O2: (1) ·
O
2
−
converted to 1O2 by rapid sequential binding with holes in the catalyst, and (2) photogenerated electrons combine at oxygen vacancies to form single-linear state excitons, which turn into triplet excitons by intersystemic crossover, and then the energy is transferred to the dissolved oxygen to generate 1O2 [81,82]. So When the 1O2 trap is added, 1O2 is constantly consumed, which results in more photogenerated electrons being used to generate 1O2. Thus fewer electrons are used for Cr (VI) reduction, which ultimately leads to a decrease in the efficiency of Cr (VI) reduction. In addition, it was determined using ESR tests that 0.4ABC was able to produce these ROS in photocatalysis. As shown in Figs. 9b and 9c, the characteristic signals could not be detected in the dark. Instead, four characteristic peaks of DMPO-·
O
2
−
(intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1) and DMPO-·OH (intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1) can be seen under xenon irradiation. In Fig. 9d, it can be seen that TEMP-1O2 has a triplet state signal with the same intensity. This indicates that the catalyst is capable of producing ·OH, ·
O
2
−
and 1O2 in the reaction.

A possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism for NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr is proposed based on XPS, ESR and photoelectrochemical experimental results. Firstly, the CB and VB of BiOBr were calculated to be −0.59 and 1.61 eV, respectively, and those of AgBr to be 0.11 and 2.48 eV, respectively, based on the results of the previous UV-DRS as well as Mott-Schottky's tests. Secondly, it was observed in the TEM images of the NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr that there was a close contact between the NCDs/BiOBr/ AgBr with a close contact interface between them, which marks the formation of heterojunction and favours charge transfer [26]. According to the EIS, PL test results were obtained for NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr with higher electron transfer efficiency than BiOBr/AgBr, BiOBr, AgBr, which indicates the formation of new electron transfer channels in the samples after the addition of NCDs [53]. The carrier lifetimes of the samples were further tested using TRPL, and the average carrier lifetime of BiOBr/AgBr was obtained to be slightly longer than that of BiOBr, which signifies the formation of heterojunction. And the average carrier lifetime of NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr is higher than that of BiOBr/AgBr. This indicates the formation of charge transfer paths in NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr with NCDs as the electron transfer medium, which further prolongs the carrier lifetime of NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr[55]. Finally, the direction of charge transfer is inferred from the change of elemental binding energy in the XPS test results. Compared to BiOBr, the binding energy of Bi 4 f in AB decreased by 0.5 eV. this indicates that the electron density in BiOBr increased after the formation of heterojunction and the electrons migrated from AgBr to BiOBr. While the binding energy of the two peaks of Bi 4 f in 0.4ABC with NCDs added decreased by 0.2 eV compared to AB. This indicates that the electron density in BiOBr becomes higher due to the presence of NCDs, and the electrons migrate from AgBr to BiOBr via NCDs [40]. Meanwhile the two peaks of Ag 3d in 0.4ABC are elevated by 0.1 eV compared to AB. this further indicates that the electron density in AgBr is reduced and there is a transfer of electrons from AgBr to BiOBr via NCDs [43]. Together, these experiments demonstrate that the overall flow of electrons in NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr is a migration of electrons from AgBr through NCDs into BiOBr.

Therefore, two possible electron transfer mechanisms were proposed: the conventional type II and type Z mechanisms [83]. If according to the type II transfer mechanism, the electrons in the CB of BiOBr will be transferred to the CB of AgBr via NCDs when NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr are excited by visible light. However, the CB potential of AgBr is more positive than the redox potential of O2/·
O
2
−
(-0.33 eV vs. NHE), so·
O
2
−
cannot be produced, which is not in accordance with the experimental results of the active species in the previous experiments. Holes in the VB of AgBr will be transferred via NCDs to the BiOBr's VB. But the VB of BiOBr has a more negative redox potential (2.34 eV vs. NHE) than that of H2O/·OH, and cannot produce ·OH [24]. This contradicts the results of the free radical assay. So NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr do not conform to the type II electron transfer mechanism. Therefore, a Z-type carrier transfer mechanism was proposed. Firstly, NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr was excited by visible light, and the electrons (e-) on the VB of AgBr were excited to leap into the CB of AgBr, while holes (h+) were formed in the VB of AgBr. Combined with the Mott-Schottky curve result we get that a p-n junction is formed between p-type AgBr and n-type BiOBr. This transfer of electrons from AgBr to BiOBr eventually leads to an upward bending of the energy band of AgBr and a downward bending of the energy band of BiOBr. The electrons in the CB of AgBr will migrate to the VB of BiOBr through NCDs under the effect of the built-in electric field, and combine with the holes in the VB of BiOBr [21,49]. Thus, the lifetime of the carriers is effectively improved. The electron (e-) on the VB of BiOBr will first jump to the defect energy level due to the presence of OVs and then continue to migrate to the CB of BiOBr when excited. The presence of OVs increases the separation efficiency of electrons and holes. Based on the Z-scheme heterojunction system, TC could finally be degraded by 1O2, ·
O
2
−
, h+ and ·OH produced by NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr, and Cr (VI) was reduced to Cr (VI) by e− reduction to Cr (III). Scheme 2.

In this study, NCDs-mediated NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts were successfully constructed.NCDs acted as charge transfer channels to enhance the redox capacity of NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr.The formation of an internal electric field between BiOBr and AgBr dramatically enhanced the electron migration efficiency. The oxygen vacancies introduced into the catalyst while constructing the Z-scheme heterojunction enabled it to adsorb more oxygen increasing the conversion of active species. The SPR effect of silver nanorods enhanced the photocatalytic activity of NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr. The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) and reduction of Cr (VI) were enhanced by about 4-fold and 6-fold. The prepared photocatalysts were capable of degrading 94% of TC within 60 min of visible light irradiation, and reducing 100% of Cr (VI) after 40 min of light irradiation.The photocatalytic activity of NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr was still high after 5 cycles of recycling, and more than 80% degradation could be achieved even in real-world wastewater. In the photocatalytic process, 1O2, ·
O
2
−
, and h+ were the free radicals that played a major role in TC degradation, while e− were the key active species that promoted the reduction of Cr (VI). In conclusion, the NCDs/BiOBr/AgBr Z-scheme heterojunction catalyst constructed in this study has important research significance in wastewater treatment, and it is also hoped to provide ideas for the preparation of more efficient photocatalysts.
